ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for plain boiled rolled oats; A00GG (oat-based porridge) is the closest defensible category anchor under the cereal and cereal product hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of plain boiled oatmeal is approximately 240 g (cooked weight), providing roughly 233 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 5.4 g fat, and 36 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Oatmeal, cooked with water — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05050017 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central NDB 08121 (Cereals, oats, regular and quick, unenriched, cooked with water) which reports energy 71 kcal/100g for plain water-cooked oats; the HPB value of 97 kcal/100g is consistent with a slightly thicker preparation or different water ratio and is accepted as the primary Singapore-context source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 for rolled oats cooked with water is a well-established value from the University of Sydney GI Database and is consistent with multiple peer-reviewed publications including Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 55 × 15.06 g × 240 g / 100 g / 100 = 55 × 36.14 / 100 ≈ 19.9 per 240 g bowl; per 100 g GL = 55 × 15.06 / 100 ≈ 8.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), zero added sugar, and 2.46 g dietary fibre with 81 mg potassium per 100 g supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Plain boiled oatmeal is well-suited for hypertension management due to negligible sodium (2 mg/100 g) and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio; patients should avoid adding salt, sweetened toppings, or condensed milk to preserve these benefits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55), zero added sugar, 2.46 g beta-glucan-rich dietary fibre per 100 g slowing gastric emptying and blunting postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain boiled oatmeal has a GI of 55 and contains no added sugar; beta-glucan soluble fibre slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise, making it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes when portioned to approximately 150–200 g cooked and consumed without sweetened additions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beta-glucan soluble fibre (component of 2.46 g total fibre/100 g) with LDL-lowering evidence, low saturated fat (~0.39 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of plain boiled oatmeal is supported by cardiovascular evidence: beta-glucan fibre at ≥3 g/day is associated with clinically meaningful LDL-cholesterol reduction; the low saturated fat content (approximately 0.39 g/100 g) and zero trans fat further support a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre at 2.46 g/100 g is slightly below the 3 g whole-food fibre threshold for positive RA benefit; no direct anti-inflammatory micronutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain boiled oatmeal is neutral rather than beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; fibre at 2.46 g/100 g supports gut microbiome health but falls marginally below the 3 g threshold for positive RA classification, and no omega-3 or polyphenol content is present — pairing with walnuts or berries is recommended to enhance anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oatmeal (boiled) is a plain cooked cereal made by simmering rolled oats in water until soft and porridge-like in consistency. It is a minimally processed whole-grain food with a mild, slightly nutty flavour and a creamy, thick texture. Per 100 g cooked weight, it provides 97 kcal, 2.85 g protein, 2.26 g fat, and 15.06 g carbohydrate with zero added sugar and 2.46 g dietary fibre, predominantly as beta-glucan soluble fibre. Sodium is negligible at 2 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is 55 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Beta-glucan content is associated with LDL-cholesterol reduction at intakes of 3 g or more per day, supporting cardiovascular health. Oatmeal is widely consumed as a breakfast staple across Western, South Asian, and Southeast Asian contexts, including Singapore where it is commonly eaten plain or with fruit. It is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal, though it contains gluten and is not suitable for coeliac disease. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management when consumed plain without sweetened or high-sodium additions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in boiled oatmeal?
Is oatmeal good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat oatmeal if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05050017, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI value of 55 for plain boiled rolled oats is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fat sub-fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 08121 (Cereals, oats, regular and quick, unenriched, cooked with water) as HPB does not report fatty acid sub-fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Core macros (protein_g 2.85, fat_g 2.26, carbohydrate_g 15.06) are all non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo; no zero-macro justification is required. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source as plain boiled oats contain no added or intrinsic free sugars; this is a confirmed zero, not a data gap. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as oatmeal is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived cholesterol; confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero in plain water-cooked rolled oats with no added fats. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as oats are a plant food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero in plain boiled oats with no fortification. 2. Saturated_fat_g (0.39), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.72), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.84) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 08121 (Cereals, oats, regular and quick, unenriched, cooked with water) as HPB Singapore Foodo does not report fatty acid sub-fractions for this entry. These values are proportionally consistent with the total fat of 2.26 g/100 g reported by HPB. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this food item. USDA FoodData Central NDB 08121 reports some of these (e.g. phosphorus 77 mg, magnesium 26 mg, zinc 0.49 mg) but as the primary source is HPB and the input record does not include these fields, they are set to -1 pending a dedicated enrichment pass using USDA as a secondary source. 4. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.07), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.03), and vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.24) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 08121 as supporting secondary data. 5. FoodEx2 code A00GG is an analogous estimate; no exact FoodEx2 code for plain boiled rolled oats was identified in the publicly available FoodEx2 browser as of 2026-04-16. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 240 g bowl: GL = 55 × (15.06 × 2.4) / 100 = 55 × 36.14 / 100 ≈ 19.9 per bowl; the field glycemic_load_per_serving reports the per-100g GL of 8.3 for comparability.
